coredata.dc:description: Introducing advanced conductive nanoparticles to combine with metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as electrode is emergingly regarded as a practical and efficient approach to improve the capacitive performance of supercapacitors. Herein, a new MOF (ZrNi-UiO-66, Nickel-zirconium 1,4-dicarboxybenzene) is designed to combine with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to form a composite electrode with high specific capacitance, in which the charge regulation is performed to facilitate the electronic conduction and transfer. Such constructed electrode delivers an enhanced electronic conductivity and an improved specific capacitance of 2468.75 F g−1@ 1 A g−1, which is four times of the contrast sample. Meanwhile, the assembled hybrid supercapacitor exhibits an increased energy density and power density, as well as a sustainable stability after 10,000 cycles with a retention rate of 91.6 %. Basing on the study of advanced characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) simulation, the mechanism of significantly improved specific capacitance can be elaborated as the promote electronic conduction caused from narrowed band gap from 3.9 eV or 0.41 eV–0.23 eV, and the increased charge accumulation at the Ni sites in designed MOFs. This work provides new insights for the design and construction of potential energy storage materials based on MOFs and/or advanced carbon-based materials.

TEM images of (a) CQD and (c) CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66; HRTEM images of (b) CQD and (d) CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66.

Physicochemical characterizations of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66. (a) PXRD patterns of UiO-66, ZrNi-UiO-66 and CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66; (b) Partial enlargement of PXRD patterns; ZrNi-UiO-66 and CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66; (c) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms; (d) pore size distribution; (e) Roman spectra; (f) FT-IR patterns.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Introducing advanced conductive nanoparticles to combine with metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as electrode is emergingly regarded as a practical and efficient approach to improve the capacitive performance of supercapacitors. Herein, a new MOF (ZrNi-UiO-66, Nickel-zirconium 1,4-dicarboxybenzene) is designed to combine with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to form a composite electrode with high specific capacitance, in which the charge regulation is performed to facilitate the electronic conduction and transfer. Such constructed electrode delivers an enhanced electronic conductivity and an improved specific capacitance of 2468.75 F g−1@ 1 A g−1, which is four times of the contrast sample. Meanwhile, the assembled hybrid supercapacitor exhibits an increased energy density and power density, as well as a sustainable stability after 10,000 cycles with a retention rate of 91.6 %. Basing on the study of advanced characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) simulation, the mechanism of significantly improved specific capacitance can be elaborated as the promote electronic conduction caused from narrowed band gap from 3.9 eV or 0.41 eV–0.23 eV, and the increased charge accumulation at the Ni sites in designed MOFs. This work provides new insights for the design and construction of potential energy storage materials based on MOFs and/or advanced carbon-based materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of electrochemical energy storage and conversion devices, including batteries, fuel cells, and supercapacitors [1–3], is motivated by realizing the efficient utilization of intermittent or volatile electricity energy generated by the weather-dependent clean and sustainable energy sources such as solar, wind, waterfall, etc. [4–6] Among these devices, supercapacitors, with quick charging and discharging processes, extended cycle life, and high-power densities, are widely reorganized one of the most promising energy storage and conversion systems [7,8]. As critical component of supercapacitors, the structure and properties of electrodes play a critical role on determining the overall performance of such device. Current widely investigated electrode materials include carbon-based materials [9], carbon nanotubes [10], metal oxides [11], conductive polymers [12], other derivatives, and so on [13–20], are widely investigated due to some advantages including low cost, large specific surface area, sufficient electronic conductivity, or high pseudocapacitors. However, some drawbacks, such as low specific capacity or poor cycling stability, still limit their further application. Accordingly, there is an urgent need to develop some new materials and/or advanced structures for electrodes.

In recent years, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), one type of emerging materials, have been explored as electrode materials for supercapacitors, due to their tunable structures, and diverse electrochemical behaviors for multi-electron reactions [21–26]. To improve MOFs' electrical conductivity and structural stability for better fitting the supercapacitors, developing novelly advanced MOF materials, or/and further combing them with some other conductive materials including graphene [27,28], polypyrrole [29], carbon nanotube [30], etc., have been regarded as an complementary strategy accordingly. However, the introducing of those carbon materials tends to stack and agglomerate on the MOF surface and block the MOF’s inherent voids, reducing the electrolyte and electrode’s contact area, or increasing the diffusion resistance of ions to prevent electron and electrolyte ion movement [31].

Fortunately, introducing conductive nanoparticles to combine with MOFs can be regarded as a practical and efficient approach to avoid those issues for improving the supercapacitors' capacitive performance [32]. As a presentative conductive nanoparticle, a group of zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles, which is referred to the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), has shown various advantages including high internal surface area, uniformly tunable pores, ideal dispersibility, good chemical inertness, excellent electron transport, as well as easily modified and assembled with other materials to form abundance of functional groups and active sites [33–39]. Considering above properties of these two materials, the combination of CQDs and designed MOFs is supposed to be an effective and promising approach to form advanced electrodes for high-performance supercapacitors. However, the research regarding such combination of CQDs and designed MOF as electrode for supercapacitors has been rarely reported.

Accordingly, herein, a new MOF (ZrNi-UiO-66, Nickel-zirconium 1,4-dicarboxybenzene) was designed to combine with CQDs to form a composite electrode (CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66) with significantly improved specific capacitance (Fig. 1 a), in which the charge regulation was performed to facilitate the electronic conduction and transfer. The obtained CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 electrode possess excellent conductivity, improved charge transfer rate, good electrochemical stability, and subsequently an increased specific capacitance of 2468.75 F g−1@ 1 A g−1 which is much higher than that of ZrNi-UiO-66 (594.4 F g−1@ 1 A g−1). Meanwhile, the performance improvement mechanism regarding the band edge position of valance band maximum (VBM) and conduction band minimum (CBM), as well as charge density distribution, had been further studied by advanced characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) simulation. Attributed to these superiorities, the assembled hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) exhibits remarkable electrochemical performance, namely a composite electrode possesses a maximum energy density of 34.79 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 750 W kg−1, and sustainable stability after 10,000 charge-discharge cycles with a retention rate of 91.6 %, showing a large potential for practical applications. Up to now, there are few reports of MOF with CQDs directly as electrode materials. The present results reveal that the hybridization of CQDs with MOFs does change the electronic environment of metal ions in MOFs, thus improving the conductivity, cycle life and coulombic efficiency of the electrodes, and provide guidance for the study of the MOF@CQDs as supercapacitor electrode materials.

All chemicals are used directly without further purification. Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (99.9 %, AR), terephthalic acid (99.9 %, AR), glucose (99.9 %, AR) Ethanol (99.9 %, AR), hydrochloric acid (99.9 %, AR), potassium hydroxide (99.9 %, AR), and activated carbon (AC) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Zirconium chloride (99.9 %, AR) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. In addition, nickel foam (NF) was used after treatment with dilute hydrochloric acid and ethanol.

CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. To explore and optimize the synthesis process of CQDs, different masses of glucose were added to 30 mL of deionized water to obtain different concentrations (1, 2.5, 5, 7.5 and 10 mmol L−1) of aqueous glucose solutions. Typically, the solution was transferred to a PTFE reactor and reacted at 180 °C, 190 °C and 200 °C for 16 h. The product was cooled to room temperature, centrifuged and then treated by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and the upper clear layer was stored to obtain a yellow clear solution for subsequent use. The optimum temperature and concentration were optimized by measuring the characteristic curve of its fluorescence spectrum. The morphology and dimensions of the prepared CQD were characterized by TEM.

349.5 mg ZrCl4 (1.5 mmol), 435 mg Ni(NO3)2.6H2O (1.5 mmol) and 166 mg terephthalic acid (1 mmol) were dissolved in 12.5 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and 7.5 mL of ethanol with a 1:1 M ratio of Zr4+ to Ni2+. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min, then transferred to a reaction vessel. The reaction was carried out at 120 °C for 24 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the product was separated by centrifugation, washed several times with DMF and ethanol and dried overnight in a drying oven at 80 °C to obtain a green powdered sample.

In a typical experiment, ZrCl4 (1.5 mmol), Ni(NO3)2.6H2O (1.5 mmol) and p-phthalic acid (H2BDC, 1 mmol) were dissolved into the mixed solution of DMF (12.5 mL), ethanol (7.5 mL) and CQD solution (5 mL). The mixed solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. The CQD@ZrNi-UiO-66was washed with DMF and ethanol sequentially, then dried at 80 °C overnight, which was named CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 (here 5 means 5 mL of CQD.). To further investigate the effect of CQD contents on supercapacitors performance, x mL of CQDs solutions (x = 0, 3, 7) were added to the solution, correspondingly to obtain samples named as ZrNi-UiO-66, CQD-3@ZrNi-UiO-66 and CQD-7@ZrNi-UiO-66, respectively.

The reagents for the syntheses were of analytical grade and used as received from commercial sources without further purification. The IR spectra were recorded using KBr pellets on a Nicolet Avatar-360 spectrometer in the 4000-400 cm−1 region. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a PerkinElmer TG-7 analyzer heated from 25 to 800 °C under nitrogen atmosphere. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns for the as-synthesized samples were recorded on an X-ray diffraction meter (D/max 2500 PC, Rigaku) with Cu-Kα radiation (1.5406 Å). The morphologies of the hierarchical derivatives were observed by using a SU8020 Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Japan). Energy dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) of different elements in different samples was recorded via SEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a VGESCALBMKII X-ray photoelectron system with an Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV).

The working electrode was fabricated by the following procedure. The CQD@ZrNi-UiO-66 (2 mg), acetylene black, and polyvinylidene fluoride were mixed in a mass ratio of 80: 10: 10 and dispersed in N-Methylpyrrolidone (NMP) to produce a homogeneous paste. The obtained slurry was coated on a nickel foam substrate (1 cm × 1 cm) and dried at 80 °C for 10 h. The as-formed electrode was then pressed at 10 MPa.

All electrochemical characterizations were evaluated using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China), and a 6 M potassium hydroxide aqueous solution as an electrolyte with the three-electrode configuration at room temperature. The as-prepared nickel foam-based electrode was used as the working electrode. The platinum wire and standard calomel electrode were employed as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. Cyclic voltammograms (CV) were recorded in the range of −0.2 to 0.6 V. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves were obtained in the same potential range at different current densities. Moreover, the long-term cycle stability of the working electrode was determined by the GCD measurement at a current density of 10 A g−1.

The hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) was assembled with CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 as the positive electrode, the cellulose paper as the separator, 6M KOH as the electrolyte, and the activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode. To achieve the optimally matched AC loading based on charge balance.

In this work, all calculations are performed using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) and the associated energies of the interacting electron exchange determined by the functional perdew-burke-ernzerhof (PBE). The calculation method is based on the Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP). The ion-electron interaction is described using the enhanced projected wave (PAW) method. A basis set of plane waves was up to an energy cutoff of 450 eV. The simulation takes into account the original ZrNi-UiO-66 and ZrNi-UiO-66-CQD models were employed to simulate.

The Monkhorst-pack method with the centered k-point grid (3 × 3 × 3) was used for surface calculations respectively. During structure optimization, all atoms were fully relaxed with the energy convergence criterion less than 10−6 eV and the force convergence criterion less than 0.01 eV/Å.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and structural features of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1b schematically shows the synthesis routes of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66, which was prepared by mixing ZrNi-UiO-66 and CQDs solution. To further investigate the effect of CQD contents on supercapacitors performance, x mL of CQDs solutions (x = 3, 5, 7) were added to ZrNi-UiO-66, correspondingly to obtain samples, named CQD-3@ZrNi-UiO-66, CQD-5@ ZrNi-UiO-66 and CQD-7@ ZrNi-UiO-66. Morphologies and features of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 are investigated by a series of measurements.

ZrNi-UiO-66 displays a favorable octahedral morphology with smooth surfaces and crystal size of 1–2 μm as observed by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging (Fig. 2 a), which is similar to the pristine UiO-66(Zr) [40]. Moreover, CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 also preserved well-formed octahedrons, as shown in Fig. 2b. The size of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 has gradually descended from 2 μm to ∼500 nm with increasing the loaded CQDs (Fig. S1). The combination of CQDs with ZrNi-UiO-66 preserves the morphology of the composites and optimizes for smaller particle sizes, which may facilitate leaching of electrolyte ions and expose more active sites. A small amount of CQDs doping effectively preserves the ZrNi-UiO-66 morphology and regulates the size, preventing its aggregation [41]. Similarly, Zr, Ni, O, and C can be detected in the mapping diagram of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 (Fig. 2c).

The synthesis conditions of CQDs are optimized at different temperatures and concentrations, and the results are also screened by fluorescence spectral characteristic curves, as shown in Fig. S2. At an excitation wavelength of 300 nm, a sharp and intense emission peak at 500 nm can be detected, which is mainly due to the absorption of the abundant surface groups of CQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of CQDs and CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 are presented in Fig. 3 . The prepared CQDs are more uniformly dispersed and ranged in size from 2 to 5 nm (Fig. 3a). HRTEM images from Fig. 3b showing the distinct lattice fringes with a 0.33 nm are consistent with the (001) planar matching of the carbon [42,43]. Combined with Raman results, the CQDs are highly graphitized and well crystallized. In addition, crystalline CQDs with a diameter of 5 nm are also observed in the TEM images of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 (Fig. 3c–d), indicating that the CQDs have been successfully loaded or embedded in the ZrNi-UiO-66.

The crystal structure of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 was detected via powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), as shown in Fig. 4 a–b and Fig. S3. The reflection peaks in ZrNi-UiO-66 (7.34°, 8.47°, 11.99°, 16.98°) are essentially indexed to the (111) (200) (022) (004) planar matching of the UiO-66(Zr) [40], respectively, implying that Ni2+ has not altered the structure of ZrNi-UiO-66. The peak pattern of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 is not much different from the pristine ZrNi-UiO-66. The low CQD content doping (CQD-3@ZrNi-UiO-66) has a negligible effect on the structure (Fig. S3a). Nevertheless, ZrNi-UiO-66 with a considerable quantity of CQDs (CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66, CQD-7@ ZrNi-UiO-66) shows a weakened XRD peak, indicating that the excessive CQDs can increase the defects and reduce the lattice structural integrity of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66. From Fig. 4b and Fig. S3b, the peaks at 7.34° and 8.47° show rightward shifts with increasing quantity of CQDs, accompanied by an increase in the lattice spacing as determined by Bragg’s calculations. Simultaneously, the diffraction peaks appear to broaden, which can be attributed to the growth inhibition of ZrNi-UiO-66 by the CQDs, leading to a reduction in size, which is in a reasonable agreement with the SEM. No diffraction signature from CQD can be observed, probably due to its good dispersion on the ZrNi-UiO-66.

Each material represents a distinct H3 hysteresis loop among the 0.8–1.0 relative pressure ranges, displayed by the properties of both Type I and Type IV isotherms [44,45] as shown in Figs. S4a–b. Fig. 4c–d gives additional information on surface area adsorption and pore structure, which features hierarchical porosity. Remarkably, ZrNi-UiO-66 provides 696.31 m2 g−1 (Table S1) specific surface area, guaranteeing a good dispersion for CQDs. CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 inherits the well-porous structure of ZrNi-UiO-66, providing a surface area (449.57 m2 g−1) that is conducive to promoting electrolyte ions to penetrate and fully contact the redox-active site, contributing significantly to the optimization of electrochemical efficiency. The pore size distribution of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 mainly ranges from 1 to 5 nm, with a few in the range of 10–150 nm. As a result, the CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 (Fig. S4a-b) exhibits the hierarchical porosity including micro-, meso-, and macropores, which may be due to the dispersion of CQDs in ZrNi-UiO-66 [46].

Besides crystal and pore structure, the organic functional groups can also be identified by Raman (Fig. 4e) and IR (Fig. 4f). Raman bands of CQD-5@ ZrNi-UiO-66 show two noticeable peaks that define the D and G bands in CQD, at roughly 1347 and 1568 cm−1, respectively, and their relative intensity ratios (ID/IG) are strongly associated with the degree of graphitization of carbon. Through comparing the FT-IR bands of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 and pristine ZrNi-UiO-66, both demonstrate comparable functional groups, Zr6O8 clusters in the 600 to 800 cm−1 range [47] and vibrations of -COOH at 1390 and 1578 cm−1, respectively [48]. The particular signatures of the CQDs are further studied comparatively. The vibrations of -CH2, which are comparable to CQD, are attributed to the peaks in CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 at 2468, 2784, and 3016 cm−1, respectively [42]. These findings provide evidence that CQDs have been successfully combined with ZrNi-UiO-66.

In general, the nature of elements, valence, and molecular structure, as well as the elemental content can be distinguished based on the peak position, shape and intensity of XPS spectra. Fig. 5 a clearly show the sample’s carbon, oxygen, zircon and nickel elements. CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 and ZrNi-UiO-66 both have similar C 1s peaks (Fig. S5a) with 284.4 eV (C-C/C=C), 286.3 eV (C-OH), and 288.7 eV (C=O), respectively [49,50]. As illustrated in O 1s spectra of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 (Fig. 5b), the O 1s broad peak are separated into three signals: the smaller binding energies (530.2/531.8 eV) for the M − O (Ni-O/Zr-O) [51] and the larger one (533.3 eV) for the O=C-O bond signal [52], showing the similar peaks except for a positively shifted with ZrNi-UiO-66, implying a slight change of electron density around O atoms [46]. Additionally, the Zr 3d spectrum of ZrNi-UiO-66 demonstrates Zr 3d5/2 (182.7 eV) and Zr 3d3/2 (185.0 eV) [53], respectively, which confirm the existence of Zr4+; whereas for CQD-5@ ZrNi-UiO-66, these two peaks shift to lower binding energy (Fig. 5c), implying a transformation in the electronic environment of Zr atoms.

Meanwhile, Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 can be recognized due to the appearance of the major signal at 856.4/874.2eV, and the satellite signal at 862/881 eV in Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 5d) to confirm the oxidation state of Ni2+ [48]. These peaks are minor negatively shifted in the CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66. The related findings demonstrated that CQDs is able to regulate the electronic structure of Ni2+, probably due to Ni2+ coordination with -OH and -COOH in the CQD. In addition, the atomic percentage (Fig. S5b) of C in CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 (49.52 %) is slightly higher than that of ZrNi-UiO-66 (45.14 %), further proving that CQD has been effectively combined with ZrNi-UiO-66.

To comprehensively evaluate Faraday’s pseudocapacitive characteristics of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 as a working electrode, a series of comparative tests were carried out by incorporating in the three-electrode system with cyclic voltammetry technique (CV), electrochemical impedance testing (EIS) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD).

At the same scanning rate (10 mV s−1), two separate, broad-symmetric redox peaks for all materials are visible on the CV closed curve (Fig. 6 a and Fig. S6), representing the reversible transfer electron process [54], indicating the typical pseudo-capacitative behaviour from Faradic redox reactions. A pair of redox peaks near 0.31 V and 0.02 V is mainly attributed to the fast and reversible redox reaction of Ni2+ and Ni3+of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66, the related discussion is presented below [55]:
Ni2+ + OH− → Ni2+(OH)ad + e (1)
Ni2+(OH)ad → Ni3+(OH)ad + e (2)

Since the closed area of the CV curves directly reflects the capacitance, it is noticeable that the CV integrated areas of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 are significantly larger than that of pure ZrNi-UiO-66, illustrating its higher specific capacitance and superior energy storage capability.

All CV profiles of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 are shown in Fig. S6. At low contents of CQDs (CQD-3@ZrNi-UiO-66), CQDs act as a conductive matrix and synergize with MOF to effectively improve the conductivity and pseudocapacitive activity. Whereas high CQD loadings (CQD-7@ZrNi-UiO-66) result in dense encapsulation of the ZrNi-UiO-66 surface and reduce the mass of active material per unit area due to insufficient exposure of the metal active sites and the blockage of electrolyte ions to enter the channel. This result demonstrates that doping with an adequate quantity of CQDs can improve electrical conductivity and facilitate the achievement of high specific capacitance.

The CV curves in Fig. S7 maintains excellent symmetry and reversibility at lower scanning rates, indicating pseudocapacitance properties, but the polarization of CV is obvious at higher scanning rates. Additionally, the CV results of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 indicate a better kinetic process between the electrode and the electrolyte with the CV integral area of the continuously grow with increasing scanning rate. In this regard, the capacitive or diffusion-controlled process contribution of CQD-5@ ZrNi-UiO-66 is investigated based on the total capacitance to accurately identify the mechanism and dynamics of the capacitive contribution according to Eq. (1) [56]. The b-value in the function is generally derived from the slope of the proportional function. By varying the scanning rate, the b value indicates whether the peak current is linearly or quadratically correlated with the scan rate. b equal to 1 is a capacitive-controlled kinetic behaviour, and b near to 1/2 is a diffusion-controlled [57]. Redox reaction b values for CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 are 0.506 and 0.532, respectively, (Figs. S8–S9), which range from 0.5 to 1, indicating a combination of surface capacitance and diffusion processes.

The percentage contribution of capacitance at different scan rates can be quantitatively evaluated by Eq. (2) [56]. The percentage capacitance contribution of capacitive-controlled processes of the electrode is gradually increased from 17.5 % to 49.9 % (Fig. 6b). This is due to the fact that the electrochemical reaction kinetics of the diffusion-controlled process is slow and it is too late for the reaction to fully occur at high scan rates, so surface-controlled process contribution is reduced [58].

Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) tests were carried out on the electrodes under constant current conditions, and then their actual specific capacity was calculated. As seen in Fig. 6c, the GCD curves presents well symmetric, indicating the high and fast charge-discharge reversibility at 1 A g−1. In particular, the discharge time of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 is noticeably longer than that of other materials (Fig. S10), illustrating its higher specific capacitance value and superior charge storage capability.

The GCD curve presents a distinct charge-discharge plateau correlating with the redox peak of the CV curve; even at 20 A g−1, the comparable shape is still conserved, suggesting a strong magnification capacity. The specific capacitance was calculated by Eq. (3). In Fig. 6d, a mass-specific capacitance (2468.75 F g−1) of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 obviously superior to that of the original (594.4 F g−1). In addition, the specific capacitance of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 attenuates by half but still far exceeds that of MOF even at a higher current density of 20 A g−1 (Fig. S11). At 1 A g−1, the specific capacitance of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 is two to four times higher than that of the original ZrNi-UiO-66, indicating that CQD synergizing with MOF indeed enhances its pseudocapacitive performance, revealing the great potential for electrochemical energy storage applications.

The Nyquist plots measured by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) are depicted in Fig. 6e, further validating the diffusion kinetics of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66. Generally, the Nyquist diagram consists of a semicircle in the mid- and high-frequency areas, which signifies the contact of electrons with the electrode interface, and its diameter represents the interfacial resistance. In the low-frequency region electrons are transported in the bulk phase of the electrode material, which appears as a diagonal line [58,59]. As for the equivalent series resistance (Rs) after curve fitting, the Rs value of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 is 0.711 Ω, lower than that of the original ZrNi-UiO-66 (1.5 Ω). With increasing CQD content, the Rs and Rct values are decreased gradually (Fig. S12), further confirming that CQD is beneficial to improving the conductivity of ZrNi-UiO-66. The capacity retention rate of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 remains 93.15 % at 10 A g−1 for 5000 cycles (Fig. 6f), higher than the original ZrNi-UiO-66 (80.72 %) and most of electrodes reported (Table S2) [60–66]. The inset graph shows a comparison of the first 5 and the last 5 charge and discharge cycles. It can be observed that the shape of GCD curve of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 is still symmetrical, indicating excellent electrochemical performance. The introduction of CQDs can alleviate the volume expansion of electrode materials caused by long-term charge and discharge cycles. Therefore, CQDs can boost electrochemical conductivity, prolong cycling stability and improve storage capability as predicted.

Basing on above electrochemical performance improvement of CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 electrode, theoretical analysis using density functional theory (DFT) calculation was used to further investigate the band edge position of valance band maximum (VBM) and conduction band minimum (CBM), which is closely related to electron conduction and transfer [67]. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after combination of CQD and ZrNi-UiO-66, the VBM of obtained CQD@ZrNi-UiO-66 increases from −5.42 eV or −3.22 eV to −3.15 eV, and the CBM decreases from −1.52 eV or −2.81 eV to −2.92 eV. The corresponding band gap significantly narrowed from 3.9 eV or 0.41 eV–0.23 eV. This result implies that the interaction between CQD and ZrNi-UiO-66 impact the band positions, thus having the potential to facilitate electron conduction, as verified in above electrochemical performance improvement of electrode in Fig. 6.

In addition to the analysis of band edge positions, the charge density differences of ZrNi-UiO-66 before and after combination with CQD to form CQD@ZrNi-UiO-66 were also calculated using DFT simulation. As presented in Fig. S13, compared with ZrNi-UiO-66, the plane-averaged charge density difference along with the z-direction is much more obvious, indicating strong charge transfer occurs in CQD@ZrNi-UiO-66 [68]. Moreover, taking a closer look at the charge density difference in local region. As shown in Fig. 7b., compared to charge density difference around Ni atoms in ZrNi-UiO-66, which appears obvious charge depletion or a small amount of charge accumulation, significantly less charge depletion or much more charge accumulation can be found at the Ni sites in CQD@ZrNi-UiO-66 [68]. This could be attributed to the Ni relevant binding energy difference amongst those two material systems [48,67], which is consistent with the minor negative shift of Ni 2p peaks in above XPS analysis.
In summary, those calculation results fit well with experimental discussions, further justifying the correlation between the theoretical models and experimental structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of the assembled supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: For hybrid supercapacitor (HSC), which use a double layer capacitor material for the cathode, and a pseudo-capacitor material as the anode, enhancing both the specific capacitances of the device and the operating voltage of the system is needed for significantly increasing the energy density. In this work, an aqueous alkaline hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) was fabricated using CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 (+) and activated carbon (−) as the electrodes to efficiently evaluate their performance for supercapacitor. The mass of the active and negative materials was calculated by Eq. (4). A standard rectangular CV curve of active carbon shows the electrical double layer capacitor from −1.0 to 0.0 V, whereas CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 from −0.2 to 0.5 V shows the pseudocapacitance characteristics (Fig. S14). A significant polarization can be seen with the window potential beyond 1.5V, indicating the voltage of the device suitable potential is 1.5 V (Fig. 8 a). Fortunately, no significant hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) can be observed at the above potential.

The double layers of the AC and the pseudo capacitance of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 can influence the CV curve shape of HSC (Fig. 8b), indicating typical hybrid capacitive behavior. As the scan rate id raised from 5 to 100 mV s−1, the shape of the CV curve is essentially unaltered and the integration area gradually expands, suggesting the better electron conduction and ion transport of this HSC. From the GCD results (Fig. 8c), both the high symmetry and small voltage drop indicate the better electrochemical reversibility. The HSC is available with specific capacitances of 111.3, 94.7, 83.3, 64 and 53.3 F g−1 as shown in Fig. 8d.

The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) were calculated by Eqs. (5) and (6). It maintains a maximum value (34.79 Wh kg−1) of HSC under the power density of 750 W kg−1 from Ragone plot shown in Fig. 8e, which surpasses the previously noted results (Table S3) [69–72]. The cycling stability of hybrid capacitors is also one of the most important indicators for practical applications. Compared to the results of recently reports (Table S4) [72–75], the initial specific capacity of the HSC is more pronounced, maintaining 91.6 % after 10,000 cycles (Fig. 8f). Furthermore, the HSCs in series can light the LED display screen and the LED glow for approximately 5 min (Fig. S15). The EIS plots of the hybrid capacitors are shown in Fig. S16. The solution resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrodes after cycling are 1.03Ω and 6.34 Ω, respectively, which are increased compared to the resistances of 0.91Ω and 6.34Ω before cycling, and the conductivity of the electrodes is slightly decreased. In conclusion, the HSC possesses a high energy density as well as superior cycling stability due to the hybrid capacitor’s intense cycle stability, wonderful multiplicity properties, and high specific capacitance of the cathode material of CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66. The results are useful for designing high energy density energy storage or conversion devices.

After a long-term charge-discharge cycle, the morphology of CQD-5@ ZrNi-UiO-66 is obviously modified from the original octahedron to the lamellar structure, as depicted in Fig. S17. Based on the XRD spectrum in Fig. S18, the diffraction peak at 7.34°is absent, while the peaks at 8.47° and 11.99° are still existed except for weakened intensity. After cycling, CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 maintains the (200) and (022) crystal planes associated with UiO-66 and forms a portion of Ni(OH)2 (corresponding to 18.52°and 32.01°) [43,73]. This may be due to the fact that OH− in the alkaline electrolyte could destabilize the Ni-O bond between Ni2+ and terephthalic acid, leading to the formation of Ni(OH)2, resulting in reliable cycling stability through the co-compatible contribution of Ni(OH)2 [76]. Meanwhile, the elemental distribution after 10,000 cycles shows the simultaneous presence of Zr, Ni, C, and O (Figs. S19–20).

The electrochemical tests data presented above indicate that CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66 performs better electrochemically than pristine ZrNi-UiO-66. Their exceptional performance is directly linked to the regular octahedral structure, large specific surface area, and the synergistic effect of CQDs and ZrNi-UiO-66. When the material is immersed in an alkaline electrolyte, the loaded CQDs not only preserves the ZrNi-UiO-66’s specific surface area and porosity but also enhance the utilization of Ni2+ in its active sites, which contributes to the enhancement of pseudo capacitance. Second, CQDs loaded on the surface of Zr-MOF with an octahedral structure are likely to form conductive chains or nodes, thus further reducing the material-to-interface internal resistance, accelerating the rate of electron migration inside the electrodes, and improving the electron and ion mass transfer efficiency, which is expected to enhance the pseudo capacitance performance. CQD-5@ZrNi-UiO-66 is one of the most attractive research objects for energy storage because of a mass specific capacitance higher than 2000 F g−1. The ideal pairing of the two electrodes is another factor contributing to the high performance of such hybrid supercapacitor.

In summary, a new MOF (ZrNi-UiO-66) was designed to combine with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to form a composite electrode (CQDs@ZrNi-UiO-66) with high specific capacitance, in which the charge regulation was performed to facilitate the electronic conduction and transfer. The optimized CQD@ZrNi-UiO-66 exhibits high pseudocapacitance energy storage properties with a mass-specific capacitance of 2468.75 F g−1 and a long cycle life, exceeding most similar materials. Besides, the assembled hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) with CQD@ZrNi-Ui-O-66 are operated with an improved power density of 750 W kg−1 and a promoted energy density of 34.79 Wh kg−1, as well as a sustainable stability after 10,000 cycles with a retention rate of 91.6 %. It indicates that the capacitive performance is related to the synergistic effect of CQDs and ZrNi-UiO-66, in which a narrowed band gap from 3.9 eV or 0.41 eV–0.23 eV, and an increased charge accumulation at the Ni sites, were obtained to facilitate higher specific capacitance. The results above will be worthwhile to construct high-density energy storage and conversion materials. As such, it is proposed that the synthesis process may be extended to the synthesis of MOF and carbon-based nanomaterials to provide inexpensive, highly active, and stable capacitor materials.
